topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The key turned in the lock with a sound that seemed impossibly loud in the quiet hallway. I froze mid-step, my hand hovering over the doorknob. Mom and Dad were standing there, beaming, their faces alight with a kind of triumphant joy that felt entirely foreign. I blinked rapidly, trying to process the scene: them, settled in their familiar living room, and me, standing on the threshold of my own life, suddenly an intruder. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. "Hey, honey," Dad said, his voice warm and utterly casual, as if we’d been planning this reunion for years. I managed a tight, strangled sound that might have been a greeting. I didn't move, just stared at the framed photograph on the mantelpiece, the glass reflecting a stranger.

[story 2]

I dropped my bag. It hit the hardwood floor with a dull thud that echoed in the sudden silence of the foyer. It was impossible. I stood rooted to the spot, my breath catching somewhere in my chest, feeling the air thin around me. They were already unpacking boxes, their movements practiced and easy, as if this was just another weekend arrangement. "We figured," Mom chirped, not looking up from taping a box shut, "it would be nice to have you around." The words hung in the air, thick and heavy. My mouth went dry. I took a hesitant step back, my eyes darting from their contented expressions to the unfamiliar arrangement of their furniture. My hands started to tremble, and I had to press them against my thighs to steady myself.

[story 3]

The sound of the front door closing seemed to seal me in. I stood in the entryway, clutching the strap of my suitcase so tightly my knuckles were white. Everything felt tilted, off-kilter. When I finally forced myself to look up, the sight of my parents sitting on the sofa, engaged in a low, easy conversation, hit me like a physical blow. My jaw went slack. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the edges of my vision swim. I couldn't form a coherent response. I just stood there, my feet feeling strangely disconnected from the floor, watching them, unable to reconcile the reality of the moment with the life I had meticulously built elsewhere.
